data.title: Freehold Royalties Ltd. 2025 Q1 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Freehold Royalties Ltd. in conjunction with their 2025 Q1 earnings call.

data.title: Fox Corporation: A Strong Quarter With Most Catalysts Already Priced In
data.description:
Introduction

Fox Corporation (NASDAQ:FOX, NASDAQ:FOXA) just delivered one of its most headline-friendly quarters in years. Revenue surged 27% YoY to $4.37 billion, and adjusted EPS came in at $1.10, beating expectations by a wide margin. But while the top-line beat was impressive, the reality is more complex. Under the surface, this quarter was less about sustainable growth and more about a one-time Super Bowl windfall that masked deeper structural challenges.

Super Bowl As A Catalyst

Let’s start with the obvious. The Super Bowl was a massive driver for this quarter, bringing in over $800 million in gross ad revenue. That alone helped push advertising revenue up 65% YoY (an unheard-of jump for a mature media company). Fox called it the “most-watched telecast in U.S. history,” with 128 million viewers across platforms. In other words, this was a record-setting, margin-distorting event, and it won’t repeat next quarter.

Even without the Super Bowl, the company noted solid underlying momentum, particularly from its AVOD platform Tubi, which posted 35% YoY revenue growth. Interestingly, this was faster than last quarter's 31% growth, despite last quarter being boosted by political ads. It means that Tubi has a nice organic growth, and we can start assuming it will continue to grow.

Segment Breakdown: Strong Revenue, Squeezed Margins

Fox splits its business into two core segments: Cable Network Programming and Television. Both performed well in terms of revenue, but profitability tells a different story.

  • Television revenue jumped 40% to $2.70B, driven by the Super Bowl and Tubi. However, EBITDA collapsed from $145M to $60M, as the cost of rights and production significantly expanded. This is where the Super Bowl paradox comes in: yes, it delivered amazing revenue, but it also dragged down margins.

  • Cable Network Programming, which includes Fox News, was much steadier. Revenue grew 11% YoY to $1.64B, with advertising up 26% thanks to strong news ratings. EBITDA rose 7% to $878M, which is a solid and sustainable pace.

Fox also highlighted that affiliate fees (recurring revenue from cable and streaming distributors) grew 3% YoY, with improvements in subscriber losses for the third straight quarter. It’s a modest gain, but a noteworthy one in a sector that’s been steadily eroded by cord-cutting. Besides, it might also suggest that Tubi is pulling in new users from outside the traditional Fox TV base, which is always a good sign.

Profitability and Valuation

Financially, Fox remains a solid operator, but the picture is more nuanced than it first appears. Profitability is a relative strength of the company, with healthy cash generation and stable margins in the core cable business. Adjusted EBITDA came in at $856 million, slightly down from last year’s $891 million, but ahead of consensus. This could suggest decent operational efficiency, and it's important to note that the year-over-year decline was driven by higher Super Bowl-related costs, not weakness in the underlying business.

The company’s return on equity remains strong at nearly 17%, and its free cash flow margin, over 12%, supports continued shareholder returns through buybacks and dividends. Management also pointed out that the fourth quarter usually brings in strong cash flow, and with almost $4.8 billion in cash, Fox has plenty of room to maneuver.

Valuation-wise, the stock trades at a discount to peers. Forward P/E sits just under 11, and EV/EBITDA is around 7.4, both below sector averages. At first, it might look like an opportunity, but it also reflects market skepticism about future growth. The trailing numbers are strong, revenue is up 27% this quarter, EBIT grew nearly 29% year-over-year, and adjusted EPS rose from $1.09 to $1.10 despite Super Bowl-related expenses. Yet when you look ahead, growth expectations drop off sharply. Forward EBITDA growth is actually negative. Analysts don't see much acceleration on the horizon, and that makes the current multiple look fair.

Fox is financially strong, but the environment around it is getting more crowded and expensive. Without big one-off events like the Super Bowl or elections, future growth will depend a lot on whether Tubi can become profitable and whether Fox One actually finds demand. If not, the stock will probably stay where it is and not go higher.

What’s Driving Sentiment: Tubi and Fox One

If there’s one area where Fox is clearly outperforming its peers, it’s in ad-supported streaming. Tubi now generates over $1 billion in annual revenue and continues to gain share among cord-cutters. According to management, 65% of its users don’t subscribe to cable, a higher ratio than Roku, Pluto, or Freevee.

Then there’s Fox One, which is the company’s newly announced direct-to-consumer (D2C) platform that will launch before NFL season starts this fall. It will bundle all of Fox’s properties, like News, Sports, and Local Stations, into one subscription product, but only for cordless users. That’s a smart move to avoid cannibalizing existing Pay Television relationships. Pricing will be “in line with wholesale rates,” according to Lachlan Murdoch, meaning this won’t be a budget option. Fox is betting on brand strength and scarcity (live sports, conservative news) to drive the demand, and not a cheap price.

What struck me is that Fox is virtuously operating in this fragile environment. It expands into D2C without upsetting legacy distributors. They even said it would be “a failure” if Fox One attracted existing cable subscribers. It’s rare to hear a media CEO actively try not to steal their own audience, but that tells you how delicately the company is managing this ecosystem.

Capital Allocation and Shareholder Returns

Fox continues to be a cash machine. Free cash flow hit a record $1.9 billion this quarter, boosted by seasonal collections and strong advertising cash flows. They repurchased another $250M in stock this quarter, bringing the total buyback since 2019 to $6.4 billion, about 30% of shares outstanding. The company has $4.8B in cash on hand and minimal near-term debt pressure, so expect the remaining $650M buyback authorization to be used soon. Here are some important quotes from the Earnings Call, said by Steve Tomsic, Fox Corporation CFO:

Turning to cash flow, where we generated record quarterly free cash flow of over $1.9 billion.

In terms of capital allocation, fiscal year-to-date, we have repurchased an additional $800 million through our share buyback program. This brings the total cumulative amount repurchased to $6.4 billion or approximately 30% of our total shares outstanding since the launch of the buyback program in 2019.

We remain committed to utilizing our full buyback authorization of $7 billion. This is supported by the strength of our balance sheet, where we ended the quarter with approximately $4.8 billion in cash and $7.2 billion in debt. And since quarter-end, we repaid our $600 million debt maturity, which came due in April.

They also confirmed that Tubi investment will remain elevated in the near term, especially after leveraging the Super Bowl for marketing. Profitability for Tubi isn’t quite here yet, but they’re hinting it’s “sooner rather than later.”

Conclusion

It’s easy to look at Fox’s Q3 and be impressed, and honestly, you should be. The Super Bowl was a win on every front. Fox News continues to dominate cable ratings. And Tubi is quietly turning into one of the most important AVOD platforms in the U.S., with real momentum behind it.

But if you step back and look at the stock’s 60%+ rally over the past year, it’s clear that most of the good news is already priced in. The market has already rewarded Fox for every possible catalyst - the Super Bowl, election-year ad spending, Tubi’s growth, and even the hope that Fox One will succeed. That last one, by the way, still feels like an “if.” There’s already pushback online about its pricing could be too aggressive, and we haven’t even seen how it performs in the market yet.

In my view, this is a solid company with strong cash flow and undervalued assets, but also limited near-term upside. Unless Fox One really takes off or Tubi surprises again, the next leg higher for Fox Corporation will be hard to justify. The valuation might look cheap, but I don’t see a strong enough reason to buy it right now. For me, it’s a hold.

data.title: Navient Baby Bond: Lock In 9.6% YTM With At Least 11% Upside
data.description:
This article was first published for our subscribers, and some of the numbers may be outdated./ Today's article is a comparison between the exchange-traded and over-the-counter bonds from Navient Corporation (NASDAQ:NAVI) and the opportunities they offer us. As the capital appreciation potential discussed in the article is around 11%, it is good to add our favorite divisibility rule to the article in case you want to learn something new: A number is divisible by 11 if the difference between the sum of its digits in odd places and the sum of the digits in even places is either zero or a multiple of 11. Now, back to the article.

Company overview

Navient Corporation is a company that provides federal and private student loans. Its total assets for the first quarter of 2025 are close to $50 billion, and the current market capitalization is $1.4 billion. Its total debt is $48 billion, and the common equity is $2.6 billion. So its total debt/ total assets ratio is 1.04, and the common equity/total debt ratio is 0.05.

NAVI has 10 consecutive years of dividend payments:

The total dividend paid to NAVI's common shareholders over the past twelve months is $0.64. Its current price is $13.87, which corresponds to a dividend yield of 4.61%. This is how the dividend yield looks for the past five years:

Bonds

Navient Corporation STRUCT INSTR PARTN 15/12/43 C (JSM) is an exchange-traded debt security with a current yield of 8.45% and a current market price of around $18. It has a 9.6% yield to maturity and years to maturity close to 20, with a maturity date of 12/15/2043. Its call date was 2008, and it is callable anytime at the moment. It pays a 6% annual interest in the form of $0.375 quarterly distributions ($1.5 per annum). It is rated by Moody's with a Ba3 credit score and by S&P with a BB- score. (Quantumonline has not updated.)

The over-the-counter bond is with a current market price of around $86.87. Its maturity date is 08/01/2033, its yield is close to 7.84%, and it is rated with a Ba3 rating by Moody's.

The two securities are different, and JSM pays its distribution to holders who have the bond on the record date, while the OTC bond trades as a regular bond, where the accrued interest is added to the purchase price.

Since they are different in type, we will use the XIRR function to compare them. We do this to have the same calculation applied to the 2 bonds.

We get that the YTM (XIRR calculated) of JSM is 9.6%, with a duration of 9.61, and the XIRR of the OTC bond is around 7.9%, with a duration of 6.43. The yields are deviated, but let's equalize them. This would happen if the price of JSM increases by 14%, or to around $21. This is what we estimate JSM is really worth, based on the idea that the OTC bond is priced correctly. If we are to give some spread to JSM for having a slightly higher duration and assume its fair value YTM to be around 8.5%, we get a fair value price of around $20

Historical comparison

Let’s take a look at how the prices of the two bonds have moved over time. When market sentiment is positive, exchange-traded bonds usually have lower yields than OTC bonds. When sentiment is negative, the opposite happens. Right now, sentiment is obviously negative, which suggests it’s a good time to buy exchange-traded bonds and wait for the mean reversion.

JSM:

OTC bond:

JSM is way closer to its 2023 low, while the OTC bond is way closer to its highs. This would have been normal based on their duration, but when we add to this the yield spread, JSM looks like the obvious "buy", both fundamentally and technically speaking. We believe that there is a good chance that they will switch places at some point in the future.

In this situation, we have two options:

  1. Direct pair trade: going long with JSM, and shorting the OTC bond.
  2. Long only: We buy a JSM exchange-traded bond and hold it until the yields equalize.

Conclusion

JSM looks undervalued compared to the OTC bond, even though both are from the same company and have similar credit ratings. JSM offers a higher yield and better return potential based on the XIRR calculation. If the market continues to favor exchange-traded bonds (its usual behavior), JSM's price could rise by around 14% to match the value of the OTC bond.

data.title: Cardiff Oncology: Buying The First-Line Onvansertib Strategy
data.description:
Finding candidate tickers to add to one of my portfolios in the Compounding Healthcare Seeking Alpha Investing Group is not difficult because it is such a target-rich environment. However, narrowing down that list to a high-priority healthcare watch list of ten to twenty tickers long can be tedious. That high-priority list is mostly filled with small-cap drug or healthcare tech companies, which are almost always in a state of flux due to volatile finances and/or pipeline developments. So, the tickers on my high-priority list are being swapped in and out regularly as their conditions change. Cardiff Oncology (NASDAQ:CRDF) has been on and off that high-priority list several times, but the conditions have prevented it from finding a spot in one of my portfolios. Now, CRDF is checking several boxes to be included in my “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio as it enters a crucial stage of development with a healthy cash position. Cardiff appears to be ready to make their mark as a potential disruptor following an interesting pivot to put onvansertib into the first-line for RAS-mutated metastatic colorectal cancer (mCRC). It is possible that onvansertib could generate over $1B in global peak sales within a few years of launch, considering the unmet need in the first-line RAS mCRC population. I am on the lookout for an opportunity to start a position in CRDF as we head into the second half of 2025.

I intend to provide a brief background on onvansertib, and provide my views on the company’s decision to take on the first-line setting. Then, I will take a look at the commercial prospects of the first-line setting and how that impacts the company’s long-term outlook. In addition, I will highlight a few notable downside risks that investors should consider when managing their CRDF position. Finally, I take a look at the Daily Chart to see if I can identify an opportunity to initiate a starter position.

Background on Onvansertib

Cardiff’s onvansertib has the potential to be a first-in-class, oral, polo-like kinase 1 (PLK1) inhibitor, which attempts to regulate tumor cell mitosis. I must highlight that high levels of PLK1 expression are associated with grim outlooks for a number of tumor types. Unfortunately, RAS mutant CRC is about 45% of all colorectal cancers and is known for being resistant to targeted therapies.

Onvansertib has an opportunity to take advantage of the mitotic vulnerabilities in RAS cancers, and possibly improve the efficacy of standard-of-care therapies, including backbone chemo treatments such as FOLFIRI and FOLFOX. However, the potential synergy with bevacizumab (an anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody) could modulate tumor hypoxia, thus sensitizing the tumors to PLK1 inhibition with onvansertib. I must stress the significance of this interaction because bevacizumab is a component of first-line and second-line treatments for CRC.

This idea was a point of discussion at Cardiff’s Type C meeting with the FDA in June 2023 that resulted in onvansertib’s development strategy being changed to first-line RAS-mutant mCRC. This makes sense because there is some rationale behind onvansertib having a superior performance in the bev-naïve patients, so there is a greater potential for tumor control earlier in the disease course.

If successful, onvansertib could redefine the standard-of-care for RAS-mutated mCRC patients.

Pivoting To The First-Line

Cardiff’s initial efforts with onvansertib were in the second-line RAS-mutated mCRC patients who had limited alternatives. However, clinical and preclinical data have shown enhanced efficacy of onvansertib in bev-naïve patients. Data presented at AACR 2024 showed significantly higher ORR and prolonged PFS in bev-naïve patients treated with onvansertib + FOLFIRI/bev matched to bev-exposed patients. In addition, the randomized ONSEMBLE trial showed that onvansertib + standard of care produced a 50% ORR in bev-naïve patients, while no responses were reported from standard-of-care alone. In vitro studies also showed that onvansertib interrupts the hypoxia pathway via HIF1α regulation, which supports the mechanistic synergy with bevacizumab. Together, these data have helped validate the company’s decision to direct onvansertib at the first-line setting.

The initial results from the CRDF-004 study were promising, with superior overall response rates (ORR) and progression-free survival (PFS) to the standard-of-care alone (Control).

Looking at the figure above, we can see that the company has two doses (20mg & 30mg), with the larger dose producing a better ORR. So, the correlation between the dosage size and the scale of effect suggests that onvansertib is the difference maker, and not the standard-of-care.

The Phase II portion of the CRDF-004 trial has already hit their enrollment goal at 41 clinical sites in the United States. The company expects an update on this study in the first half of this year, so we should get more data in the coming weeks.

However, that readout is not expected to have a mature PFS in it, so we will have to wait for another readout, possibly towards the end of the year, before we have some of the key data needed to forecast their Phase III study.

Based on enrollment being completed in April, I would assume that they could be ready in Q4, which, if positive, should provide enough insight to determine the optimal dose to be used in their CRDF-005 Phase III registrational trial.

Projecting when Cardiff can start and finish their Phase III is a bit murky, but I think it is possible the company can get alignment with the FDA in a Type B or pre-IND meeting by Q1 of next year, which could allow CRDF-005 to realistically start by Q2 2026. That being said, if they have to wait for full CRDF-004 PFS maturity, we might be looking at a Q3 or Q4 start.

If Cardiff is able to initiate their CRDF-005 Phase III trial in Q2 of next year, and is able to execute prompt site activation and enrollment, patient recruitment could be accomplished by Q1 of 2027. Assuming Cardiff can secure almost every potential FDA designation (Accelerated Approval, Breakthrough Therapy, Priority Review), my rough projection puts the earliest possible approval that could realistically occur by mid-2028, with a commercial launch in late 2028 or early 2029.

Thankfully, the company has already cited Pfizer’s (PFE) BREAKWATER trial as an example of rapid approval for a similar drug. Pfizer was able to get accelerated approval using ORR in a subset of patients from an interim time point and later reached a statistically significant improvement in PFS, which they used as their endpoint for full approval for encorafenib. Notably, the regulatory pathway used by Pfizer to pursue accelerating full approval is the same as Cardiff’s registrational trial plans that have been worked out with the FDA. Pfizer’s trial commenced in 2021 and completed enrollment in 2022, which led to approval in 2024. So, I think my rudimentary 2028 projection is appropriate.

Market Opportunity

The RAS-mutant mCRC market appears to be a substantial opportunity with ~153K annual CRC diagnoses in the U.S., and roughly 22–25% are metastatic cases. Digging a bit deeper, it looks as if an estimated 45%-56% of mCRC cases are RAS mutations, so approximately 27K to 31K RAS-mutant mCRC patients are being diagnosed. I am going to go ahead and assume that 70%–80% of these patients are eligible for systemic therapy, so we could say the U.S. addressable market is 20K-25K patients per year.

With targeted therapies often priced at $10K–$15K monthly and treatment durations of 6–8 months (no confirmation on onvansertib), the U.S. market for onvansertib could be $1.2B-$2.5B per year. Globally, with ~1.9M new CRC cases annually and similar RAS mutation rates, the market opportunity could be around $8B per year.

Admittedly, determining a peak sales projection would be imprecise, but the exercise would at least be elevating. So, let’s assume onvansertib’s pricing is and treatment duration is similar to what I stated above ($10K–$15K per month over 6–8 months), and we will give it a 20%–50% U.S. market penetration. Those numbers should get you an adjusted peak sales range from $240M to $1.5B for the United States. On a global scale, assuming onvansertib captures 15%-20% of the RAS-mutant mCRC, peak global sales could reasonably hit $1.2B to $2B per year. Keep in mind that would be for the mCRC indication… if you add in metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, small cell lung cancer, and triple negative breast cancer, onvansertib's adjusted peak revenue potential could be between $1.5B and $10.4B.

Again, this is an imprecise calculation using some industry averages and current market statistics, so the numbers are not to be seen as a passage in the Holy Writ. However, the high-end numbers do illustrate how a strong clinical profile and commercial execution could allow onvansertib to achieve blockbuster status in short order.

I will point out that at least one Street analyst has a similar outlook for Cardiff, with onvansertib achieving breakneck growth and achieving blockbuster status with a few years of a potential commercial launch.

That analyst has Cardiff’s 2033 revenue estimate at $2B, which would be a 0.09x forward price-to-sales. The industry’s average price-to-sales is about 4x-5x, so CRDF could be valued at roughly $120 to $150 per share. Considering CRDF is trading under $3 per share, I think it is safe to say that the ticker is undervalued for its projected revenue. Certainly, these are just estimates, and the company will likely have to perform some dilution in the coming years, so we can’t expect CRDF to be trading over $100 per share in the next 10 years, but again, these estimates do help illustrate the tickers upside potential if onvansertib is a success.

Risks to Consider

Despite onvansertib’s clinical and commercial prospects, investors need to accept that CRDF has several risks that could impact the ticker’s performance in the coming years. First, I must point out that onvansertib still needs to prove it can make it through the FDA, so any clinical or regulatory hurdles could be a major setback in the company’s development. My biggest clinical concern is the heterogeneity of RAS mutations with different KRAS and NRAS variants, which could dull the efficacy, and allow the tumor to employ resistance mechanisms

From a regulatory standpoint, I am concerned about the potential change in how the FDA will consider clinical trial design and requirements for approval. The company is optimistic about their pathway to approval based on Pfizer’s similar path; however, I must point out that there have been some changes at the FDA, which have impacted other companies looking for approval. I recently covered ImmunityBio (IBRX) who received a refusal-to-file letter for their sBLA despite having an agreement with the FDA over their trial design. The fear is that ImmunityBio will have to perform another trial to appease the FDA. Indeed, the circumstances for Cardiff are different, but it is possible the FDA is going to take a harder stance on clinical trial designs and endpoints going forward. So, investors need to remain vigilant concerning the protocol and timelines for the registrational trial.

Market risks will also be a major concern if there are potential delays in adoption, reimbursement challenges, as well as competitive pressure from KRAS inhibitors that could have pan-RAS goals.

Of course, we have to consider the company’s finances as we get closer to a registrational trial. The company finished Q1 with roughly $80M in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments, which they believe will last into 2027. So, it is likely the company will have to raise some cash at some point in the next two years in order to fund their Phase III trial, and possibly prepare for commercialization. Even then, the company will likely need another round of fundraising to maintain their commercial efforts, while also potentially pushing forward with one of their pipeline programs to expand onvansertib’s reach into other tumor types. So, investors need to accept that there is a strong likelihood the company might have to perform some dilutive funding in the coming years.

Despite these risks, I am giving CRDF a conviction level of 3 out of 4, and the ticker will be a prime candidate for the Compounding Healthcare “Bio Boom” speculative portfolio.

Finding a Buy

With a data update expected in the coming days or weeks, I am looking to establish a starter position in the immediate term. Looking at the CRDF Daily Chart, we can see that the share price has returned to summer-2024 levels, which provided a nice volume shelf for support.

We can see a combination of a bullish divergence followed by a high-low that bodes well for a potential reversal setup. However, I am going to wait for the share price to break and hold above the anchored-VWAP from the April high before clicking the buy button.

Once I have established a position, I will wait for the data readout before making my next move. If the data is positive, I will consider adding to the position after the volume drops, and I can identify the market’s plan for CRDF over the remainder of 2025.

Long-term, I am looking to trade CRDF to generate profit, while building a “house money” core position to be held for a long-term investment.

data.title: BBH: Fear Of Price Controls Is Not The Problem
data.description:
Introduction

Trump and team's new executive order to reduce retail drug prices is scant on details, and shifts the blame to the rest of the world for “freeloading” on US consumers who fund pharma and biotech R&D via substantial prices. The remedy offered is to have Federal Agencies (Medicare and Medicaid) buy drugs at the same prices seen in most favored nations. At the same time, pharma and biotech companies should be allowed to sell directly to the consumer and cut out the middlemen or pharmacy benefit managers (PBM), who reportedly account for 50% of the retail price. The Order placed the development of a working model and execution on RFK Jr. Department of Health and Human Services.

Fear the FDA

It seems to me, and judging by market reaction, that this initiative may not cut realized prices, revenue, or impact margins for most drugmakers. Medicaid and Medicare have the power to seek competitive prices directly from the producer, but this would require cutting out the insurance industry. Cutting out the PBM or introducing a higher degree of competition would have a positive effect on consumers, but it awaits the HHS's ideas and runs into control issues, i.e., self-medication.

The main risk to the biotech sector is most likely in getting new therapies, molecules, and drugs past the FDA and into the market. The key directors recently appointed to the FDA, CDC, and the Surgeon General are reportedly skeptical of the efficacy of vaccines and drugs and think being healthy is the key to avoiding getting sick. This may lead to reduced speed to market and stunted development, and investors may reduce funding with greater uncertainty of return.

Getting healthier, prevention, fewer drugs, and treatments should be great for health insurance companies not integrated with health care services or that derive sizable revenue from government health agencies.

What is BBH

The VanEck Biotech ETF (NASDAQ:BBH) offers lower risk exposure to this sector, with 23% of AUM in developing biotech that may be susceptible to FDA delays and pulled funding. The rest of the portfolio focuses on larger, more mature companies with established products, positive margins, and cash flow. The main risk is that the new product pipeline may suffer delays.

The ETF tracks the Market Vectors US Biotech 25 index, which has a concentrated portfolio of companies with at least 50% of revenue from biotech.

Portfolio Upside Potential

It is unlikely that analysts have reduced estimates or valuation based on yesterday's executive wish list. The consensus price targets, weighted by the current ETF composition, suggest a 27% upside potential to year-end 2025, with the top three stocks, Amgen (AMGN), Gilead (GILD), and Vertex (VRTX), that have 40% of AUM, with 14% upside. The significant potential gains, according to consensus, are in more speculative stocks such as Sarepta (SRPT), which may run the risk of FDA approval delays and lack of funding. With 23% of AUM in more speculative stocks or those with negative margins, risk-off pressure may hurt the overall ETF.

Operating Fundamentals

Consensus data shows that the more established companies in the ETF have high EBITDA margins, over 30%, but low growth, with revenue expanding around 6%. The developing companies have negative margins and could face liquidity constraints unless they already have a solid pipeline for new therapies in later stages of FDA approval. I believe that reducing retail prices should not adversely impact most biotech companies if this is done by first a more competitive and transparent PBM and direct sales to consumers.

Valuation

Surprisingly, this sector is not expensive, if we strip out the stocks with negative margins, i.e., in development. The weighted average PE is 15x with a PEG of 1.6x. These are very reasonable numbers for the bulk of the ETF's holdings. As discussed earlier, I don’t see major threats to the mature side of ETF holdings, while the Executive Order faces significant legal, operating, and political challenges. However, FDA delays may negatively impact those shares with negative margins and valuation derived from longer-term results.

Risk

The main risk to the ETF is not the executive order to reduce drug prices but rather the administration's skepticism of biotechnology's risk and effectiveness. This may lead to delays or far higher requirements for FDA approval, threatening the viability of many start-ups and companies without a deep product line.

Conclusion

I rate the BBH a sell. While the underlying holdings in mature biotech should weather the executive order's uncertainty, the company's dependence on drug and therapy approval may suffer setbacks or delays. With 23% of the AUM in stocks with higher risk profiles, the ETF may underperform the market.

data.title: Inside BIZD: An Income ETF Built Around BDCs
data.description:
What Is BIZD?

The VanEck BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA: NYSEARCA:BIZD) is a tightly focused fund that gives investors exposure to Business Development Companies, known as BDCs. These are financial outfits that make their money by lending to and investing in small and mid-sized businesses, the kind that don’t usually get much attention from big banks. It’s a niche corner of the market, and one that comes with both higher yields and more risk than traditional lending.

BIZD is one of the very few ETFs that focuses entirely on this corner of the market. It gives investors a packaged way to get into a group of income-heavy companies through just one ticker. The fund isn’t actively managed, and it doesn’t try to outperform anything, it just tracks the MVIS US Business Development Companies Index, which includes the biggest and most liquid BDCs out there. For investors who want access to higher-yield credit without having to sift through individual firms, this is the off-the-shelf option.

Who Might Consider BIZD?

BIZD is likely to appeal to investors who don’t mind trading a bit more risk for the chance at higher income, especially retirees, income-focused investors, or anyone looking to add something different to a portfolio that already includes the usual stocks and bonds. People who are familiar with how BDCs work, and who are comfortable with their particular set of traits, may find BIZD to be a straightforward way to stay invested in the sector without having to manage each company individually.

That said, BIZD probably isn’t a good fit for investors who prefer to avoid risk or who rely heavily on steady prices. Its holdings tend to move with the credit cycle, which means it can rise and fall more like a stock than a bond. While it can serve a purpose in a portfolio that’s focused on generating income, it’s the kind of investment that works best if you understand how credit markets behave and are comfortable with occasional bouts of volatility. Sharp drops and quick rebounds are both possible, depending on broader economic conditions, so it’s something that’s better used as a supporting asset rather than a mainstay in a more stable income strategy.

What’s Inside The VanEck BDC Income ETF? A Closer Look at Its Top Holdings

BIZD holds a collection of 32 Business Development Companies, though most of the weight falls on a much smaller group. In fact, the top ten make up nearly 98% of the fund, which makes it quite a concentrated strategy. At the front is Ares Capital Corp (ARCC), a major name in the BDC world, accounting for nearly 19% of the total. Also near the top are Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL), both large, well-established firms with broad exposure to private credit and the kind of scale that tends to dominate this corner of the market.

Curiously, more than 16% of the fund is parked in U.S. Treasury bills, a tactical move, likely to smooth out volatility or manage liquidity, but one that also weakens its claim to being a pure-play BDC fund. Mixed in with the main holdings are several opaque trust instruments, including those tied to SOFR-based floating rates, which suggest some degree of synthetic exposure or collateral-backed structure.

To understand how BIZD actually makes its money and handles credit risk, you have to look at its top five holdings. That’s where the real structure and strategy of the fund start to show through.

1. Ares Capital Corporation – 18.95%

Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) is by far BIZD’s biggest holding, and its role in the fund is hard to ignore. It’s one of the most entrenched and closely watched BDCs out there, with the size and reputation to match. Backed by Ares Management Corporation, it brings scale and a broad lending footprint, which gives BIZD a measure of stability. But it also means the fund leans heavily on a single player to hold things together. But having nearly a fifth of the ETF tied up in one company cuts both ways. While it allows investors to benefit from Ares’s deep pipeline of deals and institutional quality underwriting, it also means that any setbacks or credit deterioration at Ares will have an outsized impact on the ETF’s performance. For better or worse, Ares sets much of the tone for how BIZD behaves in both strong and weak credit cycles.

2. United States Treasury Bills 0.01% – 16.67%

At first glance, the inclusion of Treasury Bills in a BDC-focused fund might seem out of place, but it reflects a tactical layer within BIZD’s construction. These short-term government securities are typically used to park cash or provide a ballast against the volatility of riskier credit instruments. Given their near-zero yield, the presence of these Treasuries doesn’t meaningfully contribute to income generation, but they do signal a defensive tilt, most likely in anticipation of market turbulence or as a liquidity buffer.

3. MVBDCTRG Total Return Swaps – 13.00% and 12.27%

These synthetic positions make up more than a quarter of BIZD’s portfolio, and they’re key to understanding how the fund actually works. Instead of owning every BDC outright, BIZD uses total return swaps, financial contracts that just replicate the performance of the MVIS US Business Development Companies Index. These instruments may cut costs and make index tracking easier, but they also bring counterparty risk into the mix and add a layer of opacity that most everyday investors probably don’t see, or fully understand.

4. Blue Owl Capital Corporation – 11.47%

Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) is another major position near the top of BIZD’s portfolio, further tilting the fund toward large, institutionally backed lenders. Its lending strategy is cautious and conservative, aimed at middle-market borrowers and focused more on not losing money than chasing big returns. Tied to Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL), it has access to a broad credit platform that helps it maintain steady deal flow and spread out risk, but it also underscores how much of the fund leans on a small circle of well-connected firms. This holding gives BIZD more exposure to big-league lending platforms and adds a layer of stability. But like the rest of the fund’s top names, it’s still tethered to the volatility of the middle-market credit scene. Solid underwriting helps, but it can’t fully insulate from macro shocks.

5. Blackstone Secured Lending Fund – 9.64%

Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) rounds out BIZD’s top holdings. It’s a BDC run by one of the world’s largest alternative asset managers.. BXSL sticks mostly to first lien senior secured loans, which keeps the risk profile more conservative and pushes BIZD further toward the safer end of the credit spectrum. Being backed by Blackstone (BX) gives it scale and access to deep credit analysis, a clear advantage when markets get rough, but also a reminder of how concentrated the fund is in institutional hands. For BIZD investors, BXSL brings a layer of safer, more disciplined lending to the portfolio, without giving up on the sector’s income appeal.

BIZD Performance Overview

BIZD does what it says on the label, it gives investors high yield and focused exposure to the BDC space. But scratch beneath the surface, and the picture gets murkier. The fund’s performance and risk numbers tell a less flattering story, and it’s something investors should look at closely before jumping in. Take momentum, for example. Over both the past month and the past year, BIZD has underperformed, coming in below the median ETF in terms of total and price returns. That’s not a deal-breaker, but it’s a red flag worth noting.

Over the past year, BIZD’s price performance has been hard to ignore, for the wrong reasons. It’s down nearly 10%, while the S&P 500 managed to post a gain. And when you factor in total return, which includes those high dividends the fund is known for, the picture still doesn’t improve much. It’s lagging the broader market, plain and simple.

That said, the story shifts somewhat when you zoom out. Over three and five years, BIZD starts to look a lot better. Its total returns in those periods are not only solid but actually beat the median ETF, coming close to doubling over five years. So, for investors who can handle the bumps and hang in for the long haul, the payoff has been there, but it hasn’t come easy.

When it comes to yield, BIZD ranks near the top. Its dividend payout, north of 11%, is one of the highest in the ETF space. But there’s a catch. That big number doesn’t tell the whole story. The fund’s track record for growing those dividends is spotty at best. Over time, increases have been modest, and its 10-year growth rate trails well behind broader market averages. So while the headline yield grabs attention, the long-term trend is less impressive.

The fund’s expense profile, however, is a meaningful drag. With a sky-high effective expense ratio exceeding 12%, BIZD is among the most costly ETFs to own, by a wide margin. While some of this stems from the structure of the underlying BDCs themselves, which pass along internal costs, it’s a significant friction for total return. For a fund already facing underperformance in shorter timeframes, this cost structure makes it harder to bounce back quickly or maintain parity with broader indices.

BIZD isn’t shy about taking on risk. Its volatility numbers, standard deviation and annualized swings, run a notch or two hotter than its peers. Nearly 98% of the fund is tied up in just ten holdings, which means a bad quarter for even one or two of them can drag the whole thing down. That kind of concentration might appeal to investors who want focused exposure, but it also raises the stakes. Add in a so-so risk rating and moderate tracking error, and what you get is a fund that doesn’t offer the steadiness, or the precision, you’d find in more broadly diversified options.

Should You Invest in BIZD?

In the end, BIZD stands out as a high-yield, high-concentration income play that rewards investors willing to tolerate risk and complexity in exchange for strong cash flow. While its long-term returns are respectable and its dividend payout is among the highest in the ETF universe, those advantages come at the cost of sharp short-term volatility, weak recent momentum, and one of the steepest expense profiles in the market. For investors who understand the BDC model and are seeking income rather than growth, BIZD can be a valuable portfolio component, but only if approached with clear eyes and realistic expectations about its trade-offs.

  2. Is BIZD more volatile than most income ETFs?
